title: Default and Direct Exchanges, Work Queues, and Fair Dispatch · Contract Defense
summary: A production review compares near-miss TypeScript messaging decisions for default and direct exchanges, work queues, and fair dispatch and requires the smallest defensible direct-routing work distribution service with changed-case evidence. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for direct-routing work distribution service. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Explain the default exchange queue-name binding and choose it only when direct queue-name coupling is intentional.
Design direct exchange bindings that keep producer intent stable while consumer queues evolve.
Use one queue with multiple consumers for load distribution and state that each delivery goes to one active consumer at a time.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and TypeScript Diagnose the hidden failure · Quiz Worker Effect Evidence
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A quiz messaging team handles case 9985 in a payment-effect relay after an ambiguous provider timeout. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to combine direct routing, worker capacity, and acknowledgement-after-effect evidence; prove topology rollback before deleting the old binding; then remove the dead-letter binding and expose the resulting retention risk. Browser code models pure messaging decisions only. amqplib, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, external effects, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Acknowledge only after a durable effect or safely repeatable handoff and retain message identity, attempt, result, and failure evidence.

The visible result looks correct, but its invariant or value contract is unverified. What must happen next?
